@DiseaseEcology Actually, US is not included in our study at all, because the problems with heterogeneity. Also: we used masks mandates in the final version, but we did one of the most extensive surveys on masks wearing and in Europe the timing of mask mandates coincides with steep rises in use

@DiseaseEcology 1/2 One of the authors here. Yes, by this approach it is impossible to disentangle direct effects of interventions and signalling effect, and we state it in discussion. On the contrary it is possible to disentangle interventions from eg. global media coverage -

@DiseaseEcology 2/2 if there is sufficient variance in what countries did. Btw the model does not try to attribute whole change in Rt to interventions - from model perspective everything else is "noise term" in growth

@DiseaseEcology There are multiple countries in Europe where school vacations, some limits on gatherings and masks in selected settings are the only NPIs remaining and Rt seems close to 1, with some help of summer - definitely bodies do not pile in morgues

@DiseaseEcology 1/3 Heterogeneity: Yes in large/federative countries such US,CN,JP - all of these were not included. The most diverse was Germany, and you can see this in the holdouts experiments: real cases are lower because because state-level mandates.

@DiseaseEcology 2/3 Masks: simply put, academic demands for "rigour". We asked people how many people with masks they have seen in public spaces (as opposed to if they were masks), and personally I think the data are good in many countries.

@DiseaseEcology 3/3 The sample was self-selected, recruited on reddit - if it was a typical survey this would be a huge problem. Justifying why less so if people just report what they see / comparison with other sources would need basically whole separate paper.

@DiseaseEcology @sorenmind My guess is reproduction number just within households may be 0.6 or similar, and hard to influence. In contrast large part of the transmission is caused by super-spreading events and many NPIs influence both likelihood and size of super-spreading events, some also in private

@magnusgus @GidMK Seems hard to imagine what "stay at home only" not implying other mandates would mean - eg bars would open but with no customers, as these would be mandated to stay-at-home? I would argue the marginal difference between [set of mandates] [same+s-a-h.] is the number you want

@holmenkollin @GidMK Would be nice to disentangle schools and unis, but in principle the approaches used can not distinguish between NPIs if they almost perfectly coincide everywhere. Needs further models based on different data

@MoreauGabarain @JCedernaes One of the authors here. Actually when starting this research we hoped to include testing, but there isn't enough good data on testing and tracing. Also some of the countries doing this best have so few cases there isn't much to analyse. (We mention testing in discussion)

@MarieFleur3 @DiseaseEcology @zeynep Certainly that's not the papers conclusion. Personally as a policy advice I recommend focus on testing and tracing, moving interactions outdoors, masks, and limits on large gatherings.

Nemělo by smysl držet 5 tak dlouho až bychom byli na skóre odpovídajícím 2, a pak to zas nechat za tři týdny vyletět.

@lkristoufek @marekl To zpoždění je ale fatální problém. Index rizika extrapoluje z dat o nákazách cca za dva týdny zpět, složka navázaná na úmrtí by zobrazovala situaci zhruba měsíc zpátky a dál, to výhoda mírně menšího šumu nevyváží.

@lkristoufek @marekl (1/2) Úmrtí nebo hospitalizace by tomu dodaly víc "setrvačnosti",  jinak v různých pracovních verzích bylo třeba ještě víc bodů za nárusty, což to zase posouvá dopředu. Jsou tam jasné tradeoffs.

@lkristoufek @marekl (2/2) Ono držet ty vysoké levely hodně dlouho je trochu dvojsečné, zvyšuje to riziko oscilace: dostali bychom se do stavu kdy se rozvolní hodně, to ale bude neudržitelné, a zase to vyletí třeba až do 4ky.

Dává smysl se snažit, aby lidí v obchodě nebylo příliš najednou. To se dá dosáhnout ... lidé chodí do obchodů méně často, vyhnou se špičkám, a nakupují spíš než aby bloudili obchodem. Stejná fronta před obchodem je bezpečnější než u pokladen. https://t.co/CFxOGBFJDN

1/10 To be precise, it is asking: What worked in the first wave? It is based on data from the first wave, from 41 countries.

2/10 Basic model behind it is simple:
Imagine you would have just one country, perfect data , and just one intervention. Plotting the growth on logarithmic scale, this would be straight line, bending at the point the intervention. You could measure the effect by a ruler.

3/10 Reality is more complex. Countries applied different interventions, other factors influence the pandemic, we see just confirmed cases, there are  delays until cases are confirmed, etc. To infer the effects needs somewhat more complex statistical methods
4/10 Proper summary of the maths: https://t.co/8SEUfESZQi thanks to @MrinankSharma

5/10  Strongest interventions were closing schools and universities and limiting gatherings to under ten people. Slightly weaker is closing all non-essential businesses. Adding stay-at-home order on top of that had some additional effect on top of that
6/10 Public health measures do work, although what's the most spectacular is not always the most effective.

7/10 Strength of the approach is if you have an idea for some alternative explanation of the data,  or have some doubt about the statistical parameters used, it is usually possible to turn in into a sensitivity analysis.
8/10 When used for practical decision making by policy makers, decisions should not be based on a single study. Having said that, I would say that the results mostly agree with current literature.
9/10 Even what was somewhat controversial when we published the preprint, have become mainstream: schools without vigilant approach to stopping the spread are dangerous.
10/10 Thanks @JanMBrauner @MrinankSharma @sorenmind for all the hard work! (also @FHIOxford and @OATML_Oxford)

V žádném případě není pravda, že by Česko vykazovalo úmrtí na COVID v evropském kontextu výjimečně poctivě, a poctivé vykazování bylo důvodem, proč vychází počet mrtvých tak vysoký. 2. Také není pravda, že by na vysoký počet obětí COVID mělo jakkoli statisticky významný vliv např. hlášení autonehod nemocných s COVID. Na každou oběť autonehody s COVID započtenou do úmrtí na COVID připadá mnohem víc lidí, které sice zabil COVID, ale nejsou ve statistice.
3. Ve vykazování je ČR přesnější než třeba Bulharsko a Polsko.
4. Vysoký počet úmrtí v Česku na COVID je daný prostě tím, že COVIDem se nakazilo hodně lidí, a mnoho z nich zemřelo.

Is SARS-CoV-2 transmission seasonal? In our new preprint, we find a strong seasonal pattern in temperate Europe, consistent with a reduction in R of 42% (95% CI: 25%-53%)  from the peak of winter to the peak of summer.  Thread👇 
https://t.co/P62XUv6oCI https://t.co/xVGBxCtst0

Policy implications if a similar pattern continues:
- Seasonality is strong enough that summertime transmission reductions shouldn’t be interpreted as merely the result of immunity
- But it isn’t strong enough that we can rely on it in the absence of interventions & immunity.

One challenge for studying the role of seasonality and environmental factors is the complex web of biological and behavioural interactions, as seen in this "simple" diagram from our preprint: https://t.co/0doOjblUrT

As @mlipsitch and Viboud put it:

"This potpourri of possible mechanisms places us in a kind of Popperian purgatory, (..) data in support of every hypothesis exist, yet none of the hypotheses has been subjected to tests (..) rigorous enough to reject it"
https://t.co/ICwpK3Jx7t

In our study, we sidestep the challenge of disentangling the myriad underlying factors and instead infer a single seasonality parameter. We add a cosine seasonality term to existing Bayesian models (Brauner et al, Sharma et al), which account for non-pharmaceutical interventions.

Building on top of intervention effects models allows us to adjust for the effect of NPIs (lacking in previous work). Since both COVID-19 incidence and the presence of NPIs have waxed and waned in consecutive waves since early 2020, adjusting for their effects is crucial

We can also compare strength of seasonality to that of NPIs: Comparing with Brauner et al,  the transition from winter to summer is associated with a reduction in R that is greater than the effects of many individual NPIs, but less than the total effect of combined interventions https://t.co/7bdfT6QRxc
Seasonal differences never suppress R below 1 alone, meaning the results are entirely consistent with the possibility of outbreaks also in the summer.

Seasonality in this range also gives a parsimonious explanation of what caused Europe's 2nd wave: a "rising tide" of reproduction rate overran "barriers" of NPIs which were sufficient for summer, but more stringent NPIs were needed in autumn and winter.

Caveats and limitations: obviously we have data just from one period of seasonality, and just Europe. Long-term seasonal variations are caused by an interplay of seasonal forcing and immunity. Seasonality in other climates is likely different, as in other respiratory pathogens.

Fantastic  collaboration with Tomáš Gavenčiak, @jtmonrad, @DrSamirBhatt, @JanMBrauner, @MrinankSharma, @Sorenmind and @g_leech_. Work done at @FHIOxford,  @lshtm, @LSEHealthPolicy, @OATML_Oxford, @uni_copenhagen and https://t.co/Kcs5Xe5JbD project.

@richardneher Thanks!

We have tried with the spring 2020 data and temperature, humidity, hours of sunshine etc., but the results were generally inconclusive - while quality of weather data is great, spring testing data was noisy, and available in high resolution for just a few countries.
@richardneher We may try with fall 2020 and 2021 data in future, although the whole model gets additional complexity due to variants.
@DzungLeVN We haven't tried to disentangle whether it's pleasant temperatures, humidities, ventilation, behaviour etc - actually I find it plausible all these are important.

@IKGB10 1. As stated both in this thread and in the preprint, seasonality alone never leads to R < 1, implying the results are entirely consistent with arbitrary large outbreaks even in places where the analysis holds.
2. Brazil mostly isn't in temperate climate zone

@IKGB10 3. Estimated differences in transmission between known variants are often larger than estimated seasonal reduction. Both Brazil and South Africa are known for VOCs
4. Overall it would be great if people were able to move past "there is one explanatory factor" way of thinking
Understanding of how academia works is also useful for understanding cases when it predictably fails. https://t.co/Ul9vcqYn2c

@FriedrichPagel @ViolaPriesemann Please note the limitations mentioned: seasonal patterns from temperate Europe do not directly translate to other climates. This is also the case with other pathogens - eg it is well known India with monsoon climate has very different seasonality pattern then Europe

@Jasper_Nuyens School closures are controlled for thanks to use of NPI models. Ventilation patterns clearly have some seasonal component, so importance of ventilation is actually one of the reasons why to expect some seasonal variation in transmission.

New study from my colleagues on masks. Short summary is: mask-wearing works (mask effect for whole population would be 25%, for real wearing levels is smaller). At the same time, it isn't some sort of silver bullet. Still, from cost-effectiveness perspective, it is very good. 🧵 https://t.co/rKf52ZK0P2

The study has some advantages over previous research - a combination of:
1. measures population-level effect on R, as opposed to eg wearer protection
2. large scale dataset
3. actual wearing data, as opposed to just mandate dates
4. lots of checks for model robustness

Interestingly, mask effect is visible if you use mask use data, not mask wearing data. In the study period, in many places people used masks even before the mandate, at other places haven't really changed their behaviour even after it.

I don't think this should be interpreted as "mask mandates do not work". Something similar could be said also about speed limits - most people would drive sanely without them, some do drive at 100mph at country roads anyway, but overall they are useful.
Also, even after this study, lot of variance in mask effects stays unexplained. It would be great to understand effects of different mask types, interventions like educating people how to fit masks.

Also, for this and possible future pandemics, I believe there is large potential to develop better personal protective equipment. Ventilated masks? Printing personalised pieces which fit? Portable risk detectors based on biosensors?

New preprint on effects of massive testing, based on detailed data from Germany. Main results are: mass rapid testing works really well, is highly cost-effective, and strategies like "test every kid twice a week" are basically as good as "schools are remote". See🧵. Comments 🧵👇 https://t.co/VCpoYulazB

So... what's really frustrating about results like this, is, me and many colleagues were advising roughly this or exactly this to policymakers in CZ and elsewhere in Sep, Oct, Nov, ... finally got implemented in Mar 21.

The technology to solve large part of winter cheaply - with much smaller losses both in terms of QALYs or GDP - exited in summer 2020. There is an important and complex question why it was not used massively.

Obecné ale vážně míněné doporučení k volbám: volte, koho byste volili půl roku až rok před volbami. Pro reprezentaci vašich zájmů je důležitější co kdo dělá celé volebního období než kampaň. 🧵

Osobně si myslím, že volební kampaň poskytuje většině voličů informaci s negativní hodnotou (t.j. rozhodovali by se lépe bez ní). Výkon ve volební kampani je velmi špatnou proxy pro "dělat dobře politiku", rozhodně mnohem horší, než data "z běžného politického provozu".
Pravděpodobně by vám přišlo divné měnit rozhodnutní o obsazení generálního štábu podle výsledků zápasu ve wrestlingu. Nedělejte to v politice.

Také bych se doporučoval rozhodovat víc podle toho, jací jsou mediánoví politici a jací jsou nejlepší politici daného subjektu, a výrazně méně podle informací typu jak se chová největší magor na kandidátce.

V praktické politice mi přijde že výrazně větší vliv mají ti nejschopnější, a kvalita mediánu. Magor jistě dokáže způsobit dost mediální ostudy, ale to pro reálnou reprezentaci vašich zájmů většinou není to hlavní.

Kromě toho mám dojem, že prakticky pro české demokratické strany je netriviální se roztodivných magorů zbavovat rychle a efektivně - protože to většinou znamená nějaký vnitřní konflikt, který zas nepůsobí dobře na voliče ("hádají se").

8/9 Zpřístupnit/distribuovat kvalitní testy pro samotestování (např. podobně jako ve Velké Británii). Vysvětlovat omezenou dobu kdy negativní test něco říká.

https://t.co/ocUKRAJlcD

1/4 Problém s omikronem je, že i když toho o variantě zatím dost nevíme, není čas čekat, až budeme mít jistotu, jak velký je to problém. Jestli bude omikron v Evropě tak rychlý jak to z velmi předběžných dat vypadá, převládne už v lednu. https://t.co/fTT0OT7XaL

2/4 Pokud zároveň s rychlým šířením dokáže dost dobře obejít imunitu, pořád hrozí velké problémy: prostě proto, že se moc lidí nakazí naráz, což může přetížit nemocnice, rozvrátit výuku, omezit provoz ve firmách, a tak dále. (A hrozí to i pokud by průběhy byly o trochu lehčí.)

3/4 Nic z rizik není jisté, ale je potřeba se připravit na horší možnosti, i když mají pravděpodobnost třeba jen 20%. Pokud nám bude přát štěstí a nakonec nastane nějaký lepší scénář, můžeme být rádi, ale nelze na to vsadit. Náklady na přípravu jsou minimální oproti riziku.

4/4 Zkušenost ukazuje, že stát neumí spoustu věcí zařídit rychle, například něco nakoupit - proto je třeba se připravovat teď, a ne až se bude omikronu 50%. Srozumitelná otevřená komunikace rizika by také umožnila přípravu firmám, školám, nemocnicím atd.

Spekulativní:
Přijde mi dost pravděpodobné (tak 85%), že omikron má rychlejší průběh křivky nákazy (viz náčrtek), a kratší sériový interval mezi nákazami. To by znamenalo řadu věcí 🧵 https://t.co/flSjomKuTq

2/6 Samozřejmě, dřívější schopnost někoho nakazit. Přijde mi možné, že u zvlášť rychlých náběhů by mohla být část lidí nakažlivá třeba už za den od nákazy. (Například: ve čtvrtek ráno se v práci nakazíte sami, na páteční večeři už jste infekční)

3/6. Kratší sériový interval znamená rychlejší růst počtu nákaz při stejném reprodukčním číslu (ekvivalentně: kratší čas zdvojnásobení). To má i pozitivní stránku - hranice, kdy se epidemie sama zastavuje “promořením”, závisí na reprodukčním čísle, ne sériovém intervalu (zhruba).

4/6 Tedy vlna epidemie viru s kratším intervalem rychle roste, ale zastaví se o něco dřív, než by se zastavila na začátku stejně rostoucí vlna, kde by rychlost byla daná delším intervalem a vyšším reprodukčním číslem. A pak zase rychle klesá!

5/6. Důsledky pro strategii testování a trasování. S rychlejším průběhem by rostl problém s prodlevou mezi odběrem a výsledky PCR testů a rostla užitečnost antigenů. Také by bylo potřeba pozměnit priority trasování - hodnota dotrasování třeba týden starých případů je minimální.

6/6. Pokud se zrychlení potvrdí, stálo by za zvážení zkrátit dobu izolace (a asi neprodlužovat karantény), a delší čekání nahradit testováním. Problémem vlny omikronu může být velké množství nakažených - např. několik set tisíc lidí za den způsobí problémy i s lehkým průběhem.

Poznamenávám, že rychlejší vývoj virové nálože u omikronu jsem zatím naměřený a publikované neviděl - co mě k odhadu "rychlejšího průběhu" vede je, že dobře odpovídá řadě nepřímých indicií.
(náčrtek je ilustrační, vertikální osa logaritimická)

Pardon, ale tohle je maximálně zavádějící vyjádření, které u lidí pouze vyvolává nesmyslně negativní představy o antigenním testování. Podrobné vysvětlení ve 🧵. Zmíněné číslo "65% nezachycených" závisí hlavně na tom, kdo se testuje. Stejně by šlo dojít třeba k "5%" nebo "90%" https://t.co/a0TfUBbOue

1. Kvalitní ag testy detekují dost spolehlivě lidi s vysokou virovou náloží (nízké hodnoty Ct). Virová nálož zároveň koreluje s infekčností - to znamená, že testy "vychytají" nejlíp ty nejinfekčnější.

2. Vzorky s Ct hodnotou třeba 30 sice znamená osoby "pozitivní", ale pokud jsou to vzorky od lidí na sestupné části křivky virové nálože, jsou většinou v době výsledku testu infekční málo nebo vůbec. https://t.co/SmC8zrp1FS
3. PCR test u takových lidí má sice diagnostickou hodnotu, ale z hlediska omezení šíření nemoci to důležité není. Že v Ag testu vyjdou "negativní" lze považovat i za výhodu.

4. Citlivost PCR by byla výhodou, kdybychom pomocí PCR chytali lidi na "roustoucí" části křivky, a výsledky PCR byly zhruba do 12 hodin (vzhledem k rychlosti omikronu). To pro naprostou většinu PCR testování v Česku vůbec neplatí, lidé se na test dostanou dny po začátku příznaků.

5. Samozřejmě podle toho jak je poskládaný vzorek koho testujete, může vám ve srovnání Ag vs PCR vyjít cokoli mezi 5% nezachycených 95% nezachycených. Jako informace je to k ničemu a pro veřejnost je to maximálně matoucí.

2/6 ...with talks from some of the people working on cutting edge research such as @rohinmshah (@DeepMind ) @stanislavfort (@AnthropicAI) @DavidSKrueger (@Cambridge_Uni) @OndrejBajgar (@FHIOxford) @lrhammond (@coop_ai) @AmmannNora (@pibbssai) & more. Hosted by @CTS_uk_av
3/6 Who is the school designed for? Researchers interested in learning more about the AI alignment topics, PhD students, researchers working in ML/AI outside academia, and talented students.

@Ollie_Base (1/4) My impression is this seems mostly missing what my concern about the tradeoffs is. Roughly speaking 2-person clusters compete for "energy" with different cluster sizes - trios, small groups, larger groups, "whole event".

@Ollie_Base (2/4) In my view, the current emphasis on 1:1s sucks the energy from most of these higher-order levels of organization, except the top one ("whole event"). So you get a lot of energy in people networking on 1:1 basis, and second "the whole event", which is roughly "EA crowd".

@Ollie_Base (3/4) If you straightforwardly count the "value" of the event in the number of 2-person links, eg by asking "how many people you know now" or similar network metric, you will find 1:1s are just amazing. To be clear: I do agree some amount of networking is valuable

@Ollie_Base (4/4) What I'm worried about ~is:1) you don't understand the value of n-people interactions 2) the value is not captured by the metrics like "count people now in your network" 3) sources of value which are harder to measure/understand get crowded out. Important things may be lost

@Ollie_Base I was also a first-timer at EAGxs; based on my experiences at EAGx Oxford 2016 I guess I got more from some small-group conversation with @anderssandberg than what the counterfactual would have been if the 1:1 culture was as strong then [low confidence, counterfactuals are hard]

@Meaningness @fchollet 1/n The main difference is in "understandability to humans". Plain GPT, which only predicts, has no intelligible "identity".  You present him with a text that looks like angry dialogue? It will predict the sequel. You present the beginning of a contract? He'll finish the contract

@Meaningness @fchollet 2/n  Empirically, it seems that most people find it hard to interact with such a system. Even as humans we are predictive-processing systems, and it is easier for us to interact with something we can "predict" or understand what type of conversation we are in

@Meaningness @fchollet 3/n Interactions like "I'm going to go to the bakery. The clerk says hello. I ask for half a loaf of bread. The shopkeeper asks what kind. I say sourdough. The clerk says 45 crowns. I say card. I pay. I say thank you. I say goodbye." are useful and predictable

@Meaningness @fchollet 5/n Realistically, most people most of the time are much more interested in interactions where it's at least roughly understood what the other party's role in the conversation is... Voila, "ChatGPT", GPT prompted and fine-tuned to have a role and an "identity".

@Meaningness @fchollet 6/n Specifically, prompted and fine-tuned to play the role of the helpful, servile and a bit boring "AI assistant". Able to answer "who are you". That's the core difference making ChatGPT popular - it's human-understandable role, and high-level "predictability"

Short high-level piece on AI strategy: we should expect pivotal "cyborg periods" where human+AI teams are more powerful than humans, and also notably more powerful than AIs alone
 https://t.co/yebEROkoKv

2/7: The cyborg period in any domain will increase capabilities and the % of automation, leading to increased progress. Strategically important domains include AI research, AI alignment research, human coordination, and cultural evolution. & more
3/7: The timing of transitions in different domains is not independent. Order of transitions matters. There are different possible winning strategies for decreasing risks from AI.

4/7: Leveraging the power of human+AI teams during cyborg periods is critical for navigating transitions to the next period of very advanced AIs. Practicing working in human+AI teams in the sub-domains where automation is more advanced is important.
5/7: Als: I'm quite sceptical about our ability to accurately predict the trajectory we will take, so becoming good at course correcting is important.

6/7 Also, we notice a bias where people coming from different backgrounds may perceive their domain's increases in power as the decisive transition, but underestimate the impact of transitions in other domains on their domain.

7/7 In short: for most practical purposes, it's useful to be more specific rather than just talking about concepts like "date of AGI". Models should be as simple as possible, but not simpler, and single-dimensional "AI progress" with "date of AGI" is not the optimum.

Possibly the most harmful public dispute happening now:

The fight between researchers concerned about the immediate harms posed by existing AI technology versus those concerned about its potential trajectory in the coming years or decades.

Why is this unfortunate and confused?…

Possibly the most harmful public dispute happening now:

The fight between researchers concerned about the immediate harms posed by existing AI technology versus those concerned about its potential trajectory in the coming years or decades.

Why is this unfortunate and confused? The core technical issues linked to short-term risks often scale to existential threats, predicated on the rise in AI abilities.

Consider the imminent threat of large language models (LLMs) being utilised for broad-scale manipulation. Theoretically, this can be viewed as AIs influencing human culture towards whatever is the result of training.

Now, imagine AI systems increasing in capability, with the majority of written words being generated by AI assistants and human culture becoming increasingly running on AI. It's not hard to conceive a future where such machine-guided cultural evolution leads to x-risk outcomes, such as driving human societies crazy, or manipulating them into yielding control to AI systems.

Take another example, the contemporary problem of AI systems producing uninterpretable decisions outcomes in areas like healthcare or justice. At a basic level, the problem is it's much simpler to train a system to perform a task than to generate a human-understandable explanation for its actions.

Now, imagine a world where competitive forces drive corporations, governments, and even individuals to delegate an ever-growing proportion of decision-making to AI systems. It's not difficult to foresee this leading to x-risk levels of information asymmetry, where humans lose control due to the complete incomprehensibility of an AI-dominated world.

Or, there are very specific concrete incidents, like the Bing chatbot writing threats to users. Imagine this issue magnified with a much smarter system engaging in millions of conversations. Is it hard to imagine, if you were able to run a million copies of yourself, working as a "helpful virtual assistant", this would give you power to actually blackmail people, or stage a coup?

While I can only speculate about the reasons behind this situation, here are some guesses:

1. The most extreme voices on both sides tend to be amplified. My personal observation is that the stance "immediate risks are compatible with scaled-up risks" is actually quite common.…

While I can only speculate about the reasons behind this situation, here are some guesses:

1. The most extreme voices on both sides tend to be amplified. My personal observation is that the stance "immediate risks are compatible with scaled-up risks" is actually quite common. However, an aggressively dismissive attitude often garners more attention.

2. Unfortunately, personal animosities and grievances seem to also play a part. Although these factors should not influence public discourse, they do.

3. Clustering and polarization effects in networks, leading to emergence of distinct communities under labels like "AI safety", "AI ethics" or "AI social impacts".

Lesk a bída narativní úrovně analýzy / crosspost

Před nedávnem jsem upozorňoval na určitou marnost čistě "sociologické" nebo dokonce "religionistické" analýzy debat o rizicích AI, pokud je zcela odtržena od chápání technické podstaty problému. Shodou okolností nedávno vyšel článek který tenhle typ marnosti demonstruje na tématu, které je analyzovatelné mnohem snáze: vakcíny a covid. 
Konkrétně jde o text "Covid Vaccination Disputes in Czechia: Political Myth-Making and Boundary Work" který napsal religionista, duchovní a filozof doc. Radek Chlup a vyšel v časopise Minerva
Téma, kterému se článek věnuje, je česká kvazi-vědecká scéna soustředěná kolem sdružení SMÍŠ, ZF a podobně. Autor tyto weby celou pandemii pečlivě sledoval a podle mého dojmu z části přijal jejich narativy. Podle vlastního dojmu se chápe jako nezávislý výzkumník povznesený nad věc samotnou. Střet popsaného okruhu s mainstreamovou vědou proto popisuje jako střet “pro-vax” a “free-vax”. 
Text Radka Chlupa je svým způsobem brilantní: rozebírá covidovou diskusi na úrovni tvorby mýtů, odhaluje propojení veřejné diskuse o vakcinaci s “morálními apely”, rozebírá roli médií, a poskytuje řadu postmoderních vhledů do toho, jak část médií vnímá jako vlastní poslání nejen reprezentovat všechny názory, ale i oddělování pravdy a nepravdy. 
Na úrovni mýtů a narativů je to poučné. Trochu problém je, že autor nechápe přízemnější, “biologickou”, “medicínskou” a “statistickou” úroveň problému. To mimochodem není triviální - už v průběhu pandemie jsem v textu “Problém symetrie” upozorňoval, že pokud nemáte dobré epistemické základy, nebo nemáte expertní znalosti Bayesovské statistiky, nemusíte mít snadnou možnost poznat co je pravda, a co ne. Například na jedné straně máte celkem libovolného profesora epidemiologie, na druhé prof. Berana, ale pokud epidemiologii nebo středoškolské matematice nerozumíte, je problém symetrický - profesor epidemiologie vs. profesor epidemiologie, oba jsou experti! 
Pokud v tomto “facts-blind” pohledu pokračujete v analýze, například k otázce proč část médií přestala prof. Berana zvát, vás jako nejpřirozenější hypotézou napadne  … marginalizace menšinového názoru, byť jej hájí uznávaný expert. (“Although the free-vax position was marginalized in Czechia (as elsewhere), it was publicly supported by a number of established scientists and medical experts working at various state institutions. The best known among the general public is Jiří Beran, epidemiologist and vaccinologist, director of the Vaccination and Travel Centre”)
Pokud otázku “jak to je doopravdy” dáte do závorky, nebo neumíte odpovědět, soubojem narativů, mýtů, a ideologií se stane všechno. Radek Chlup například jako jeden ze tří “ideologických principů” tzv. “pro-vax pozice” uvádí “The acceptance of vaccination as the main solution of the pandemic was generally seen as equal to embracing scientifc consensus, to rejecting “myths” in the name of “facts”.” Zároveň podle Chlupa “Free-vax scientists were just as rational as their pro-vax colleagues.”  
Problém je že … ne, nikoli. Účinnost vakcín nebyl jen “mýtus” nebo “narativ”.  Na učitečnosti vakcinace skutečně existovala široká shoda, nejen “narativ shody”. Ne,  skutečně není pravda, že experti jako Hana Zelená, Zuzana Krátká nebo Tomáš Fürst jsou “stejně racionální” jako kdokoli jiný. Není to pravda ve stejném smyslu, jako není pravda, že příznivci hypotézy placaté stacionární Země jsou stejně racionální jako příznivci hypotézy kulaté rotující Země. 
Pokud se v ohisku vašeho pohledu ocitně přesně zaostřená vrstva ideologií a narativů, a jevy se snažíte vysvětlovat převážně hypotézami této vrstvy,  můžete dojít k závěru, že hypotéza o placaté stacionární Zemi je v naší společnosti potlačována u dětí už od útlého věku. Její příznivci jsou předmětem šikany, jsou vytlačeni daleko za hranice mainstreamu a vědy. Víra v placatost země je považována za etalon iracionality, a je předmětem zesměšnění ještě mnohem více než “free-vax”. Její příznivce médií nezvou, nebo pokud už, spíše do zábavných pořadů. A přitom: pokud se na věc podívate dostatečně úzkým pohledem religionisty, jsou příznivci Země kulaté zhruba stejně racionální, jako běžní příznivci Země placaté. Sami si můžete vyzkoušet: když se příznivců kulaté Země zeptáte na Facebooku kdo vůbec četl poslední výzkumy o placatosti Země, dočkáte se úšklebků místo argumentů. Obojí je legitimní hypotéza, a kdo z vás osobně provedl nějaké experimentální měření zakřivení povrchu zemského?
Aby nedošlo k omylu: Myslím si, že analýza na úrovni narativů, memů a kultury pohled na celou situaci může výrazně obohatit a je cenná.  Nicméně pokud se spolehnete _jen_ na ni, a přízemní, “fyzikální” či “biologickou” rovinu věci ignorujete… hrozí, že vám unikne celá důležitá třída vysvětlení. Například, že hypotéza o kulaté Zemi je prostě lepší model tvaru Země. Nebo, v tomto případě, že důležitým vysvětlením konsensu o účinnosti vakcín a zdrojem “pro-vax” narativu byla ... účinnost a bezpečnost vakcín.

Is Meta really all-in on open source, or is there something else at play?

I think you should consider this hypothesis:
- Meta doesn't actually embrace open source.
- Meta has a sensible, self-interested strategy pursued when advocating for minimum regulation and maximum AI proliferation.
Unfortunately, in my view, the likely goal is quite dystopian.

To understand Meta, it's prerequisite to comprehend the "commoditize the complement" strategy. This is a business approach where a company aims to drive down the cost or increase the availability of goods or services complementary to its own offerings. The outcome is an increase in the value of the company's services.

Some famous successful examples of this strategy include:
-Microsoft and PC hardware: PC hardware became a commodity, while Microsoft came close to monopolizing the OS, extracting huge profits.
-Apple's App Store: The complement to the phone is the apps. Apps have become a cheap commodity under immense competitive pressure, Apple the most valuable company in the world

Meta itself itself commoditized content: content is the complement to their platform.

The future Meta likely aims for is this:
- Meta becomes the platform of virtual reality.
- People basically move there
- Most of the addictive VR content is generated by AIs, which is the complement.

For this strategy to succeed, it's quite important to have a thriving ecosystem of VR producers, competing on which content will be the most addictive or hack human brains the fastest. Why an entire ecosystem? Because it fosters more creativity in brain hacking. Moreover, if the content was produced by Meta itself, it would be easier to regulate.

Why AIs? Currently, VR content production is too labor-intensive to produce at scale. (Also, VR hardware is a bit annoying, but if the hardware improves, and the content is addictive enough, this is not an insurmountable barrier.)

Pro páteční Události, komentáře po domluvě Petr Salaba vytvořil názornou ukázku možností syntézy hlasu. Doporučuju, nutné se zvukem. Jana Peroutková to ustála bravurně, ale ono je to fakt dost znepokujující, když vaším hlasem slyšíte něco, co jste neřekla. 

Jinak, pejskové a kočičky, trochu vážněji

1. Zneužití téhle technologie pro phishing, kdy někdo hlasem vnoučat, příbuzných,nadřizených, kolegů  a podobně zavolá a bude chtít nějaké přístupové údaje, něco někam poslat, zadat, nainstalovat nebo udělat nějakou akci ... je celkem zjevná věc. Jako lidi jsme dosud docela zvyklí využívat schopnost identifikovat hlas někoho koho známe jako autentizační prostředek. Nebyl to dobrý nápad ani dřív, ale je s tém fakt třeba přestat.

2. Úplně minimálním opatřením, na kterém by měly státy zapracovat, je povinnost opatřovat vodoznakem a často i přímým upozorněním libovolné výstupy AI simulující lidi - text, hlas, obraz...  Samozřejmě se proti tomu objeví defaultní protiargumenty typu dostatečně schopný zločinec si cestu k neoznačkovanému modelu najde, a co Čína, atd. Reposting on Twitter... I don't know what's going on with @sama. But some context seems to be lost on many on Twitter.

What led OpenAI to where it is today?

OpenAI was not created with the goal of "we will be the next unicorn and make billions of dollars." Rather, the goal was "we will solve AGI for the benefit of humanity."

_This mission_ has allowed OpenAI to hire some of the smartest people alive today. Without naming names, some are on the level of Feynman in terms of genius. In time, you'll read about them in textbooks.

Contrary to popular belief about what motivates people, you don't get talent this high just by paying them well or offering a vision of building the next ten billion dollar business. For some, "making more money on products" is ...just not that extremely interesting. Historically, many of these people have been drawn to fields theoretical physics, but AGI and AI safety is the challenge of our age.

OpenAI was created as a non-profit lab to work on AGI.

Sam Altman certainly contributed to OpenAI's success, but in my view the main capital that brought OpenAI this far is not management genius. Rather, it is the technical genius and vision of the people recruited to OpenAI, attracted not by money but by advancing AGI safely.

How I can tell? Well, some of these people left OpenAI before, believing it was optimizing too much for money rather than safety. They started Anthropic... now OpenAI's biggest competitor at a $30 billion valuation.

It's probably worth noting that "AGI" is not being built by individual humans, but by powerful non-human agentic entities - you can call them different names, like collective agents, memeplexes, egregores, or even in some sense corporations.

From this perspective:
- OpenAI is not just the legal entity, or employees, but this non-human superagent
- OpenAI staffers tweeting "OpenAI is nothing without its people" are partially confused - the enitity relies on them by running on their brains, though it also has more power over them than they see and admit
- clearly, there is some misalignment between "the board" and the entity
Broadly, we don't know how to keep these entities human-aligned, and one of the major, not clearly recognized challenges of AI safety is how to solve this. 
No matter how the OpenAI drama unfolds, I've become a little bit pessimistic about our ability to solve this in time
To be clear: in mortal danger, the entity reconstituting under MS corporation likely counts as survival

In the social media circus, discussions about AI risk often turn into a game of 'Spot the Cult'. The problem I have with this is it tells you very little, if anything, about the underlying issue. Take climate activism: parts of it are cultish, but you can't infer much about…

In the social media circus, discussions about AI risk often turn into a game of 'Spot the Cult'. The problem I have with this is it tells you very little, if anything, about the underlying issue. Take climate activism: parts of it are cultish, but you can't infer much about global temperatures from this observation. Or anti-vax movement: parts are cultish, and the underlying issue mostly does not exist - risks of vaccines are minimal compared to their benefits. Animal rights movements has cultish elements, but does imply the suffering of factory-farmed animals does not exist? Some startups feel like a cult, but that doesn't predict success or failure. 

The problem here is ...almost any way how to coordinate humans at scale has some similarity to religion. If the humans need to act somewhat coherently, it often involves shared beliefs and some communication. Shared beliefs and communication are subject to selection pressures of cultural evolution, so you get something which fits the concept of 'memeplex'. This results in a dynamic where individuals are, in a sense, 'serving the ideas'.

Noticing this, and how is this similar to religion… unfortunately, tells you approximately nothing about the factual claims. We observe this same dynamic across claims both true and false, and ideologies that lead to human flourishing as well as disasters. The mere fact that people coordinate around an idea in this way is not a strong evidence for or against its validity. 

The inconvenient truth in the age of social media is... to judge if something makes sense or not you need to actually look into the claims and arguments.

Excited to share our new paper: "Predictive Minds: LLMs As Atypical Active Inference Agents" accepted at #NeurIPS23 SoLaR workshop. We look into the gap between LLMs and humans using the frame of active inference. Our short answer: the gap is smaller than people think! We also use the frame to shed light on model hallucinations and the possible emergence of self-awareness in generative AI. 🧵

Active inference is a theory from cognitive science/neuroscience that tries to explain biological systems like brains. ActInf systems (like brains) fundamentally aim to minimise the difference between their predicted and actual sensory inputs by constantly updating their internal…

Active inference is a theory from cognitive science/neuroscience that tries to explain biological systems like brains. ActInf systems (like brains) fundamentally aim to minimise the difference between their predicted and actual sensory inputs by constantly updating their internal models through interactions with their environment. . Our paper posits that LLMs like GPT, traditionally viewed as passive predictors, or even 'stochastic parrots', could come to fit this active inference paradigm.

While LLMs currently lack a tight feedback loop between action and perception, they align with the active inference paradigm in other ways, such as learning a world model, or general sequence prediction abilities

This sheds some light on the problem of hallucinations. Active inference assumes human perception is also  'constrained hallucination', constantly synchronised to reality by sensory inputs. But notice the difference: our sensory inputs are contextual, first person. In contrast,…

This sheds some light on the problem of hallucinations. Active inference assumes human perception is also  'constrained hallucination', constantly synchronised to reality by sensory inputs. But notice the difference: our sensory inputs are contextual, first person. In contrast, LLM training data are contextless, a bit like if you hearing conversations of random people sampled from diverse places and times. If you were in such a context-less situation, you would also tend to 'hallucinate' more, simply because of the need to entertain many hypotheses about context. For instance, we illustrate this with the example of predicting a continuation for the phrase "And she drew her sword and exclaimed 'Heretics must die!'" - hearing this without context, you would be uncertain if you are in a mediaeval setting, a fantasy tale, or a video game scenario. This contextual ambiguity contributes to some hallucinatory responses of LLMs. Another explanation, from research by Ortega (Shaking the foundations: delusions in sequence models for interaction and control. arXiv preprint arXiv:2110.10819), is the lack of distinction between action and perception states - simply put, LLMs are confused about what is "their" action and what comes from the outside. What's fascinating is that human brains can be dysfunctional in a related way, as in the psychiatric condition known as 'illusion of control'.

A key insight is the potential for LLMs to act in the world: while they lack direct actuators, their output can still affect the world in significant ways - and these can be viewed as a sort of action. Current training regimes lack direct links between these actions and LLMs'…

A key insight is the potential for LLMs to act in the world: while they lack direct actuators, their output can still affect the world in significant ways - and these can be viewed as a sort of action. Current training regimes lack direct links between these actions and LLMs' 'perception', but this gap might close soon, leading to more adaptive, self-aware AI.

Building on previous research on importance of situational awareness (Berglund et al. https://t.co/u5WybXJqp0), we look into the question of self-awareness. We suggest that the intensity and speed of the feedback loop between systems actions and training is the key driver of…

Building on previous research on importance of situational awareness (Berglund et al. https://t.co/EMFo7JZsmT), we look into the question of self-awareness. We suggest that the intensity and speed of the feedback loop between systems actions and training is the key driver of self-awareness, rather than mere scale.

Consider a thought experiment: imagine a human receiving visual input from thousands of security cameras trying to self-localize. The task is much simpler if she can act, e.g. waving a hand and correlate this to hands shown in some of the camera feeds. Similarly, as AIs’ feedback loop between action and perception tighten, they might develop an enhanced sense of self by understanding the effects of their outputs on the world.

@bindureddy This is misinterpreting the paper and making claims unsubstantiated in the paper.

1. People use quite different definitions of emergence. Emergence in the sense that macroscopic behaviour of the system is hard to understand from looking at microscopic details holds for LLMs and…

This is misinterpreting the paper and making claims unsubstantiated in the paper.

1. People use quite different definitions of emergence. Emergence in the sense that macroscopic behaviour of the system is hard to understand from looking at microscopic details holds for LLMs and the paper does not really claim much about the "micro"-"macro" predictability.

'Emergence' is used in more more narrow/specific meaning of  "abilities that are not present in smaller-scale models but are present in large-scale models"

2. The core of the debate basically is about how 'discontinuous' are transitions in LLMs, and also, what sort of metric is relevant. The core claim of the paper is many of the discontinuities observed are because of threshold effects / discontinuities in the metric, and using smoother metric illustrates underlying continuity.

3. While this is a good point and valuable contribution to understanding of scaling behaviours, the real world impact of the finding is limited. Mostly because real world tasks or impacts often also have somewhat discontinuous nature. For example, in the spirit of the paper, the sequence of answers to 17x34=  111; 158 ; 468 ; 488;  578 ... may be interpreted as "smooth improvement" in multiplication task, using edit distance as a metric. However, only the last answer is arithmetically correct, and for many practical purposes this is what you care about.

4. This gets more pronounced in various composite or complex tasks. Imagine a capability which depends on the model getting the multiplication right in the first step, and using the result in a second step. While the edit distance to the correct numerical value may be decreasing gradually, the overall performance on the composite task will be discontinuous when the.

5. 'Free will' is a pure red herring to derail the discussion. No common arguments about AI risk use the concept at all.

6. It is easy to imagine dangerous AI capabilities where the discontinuity is 'in the world', similarly to arithmetics. 

7. We have already have examples of current models exhibiting dangerous or misaligned behaviours, such as gaslighting users, sycophancy or  threatening the user. Also we have speculative examples of increased self-awareness or situational awareness of LLM agents. If anything, the paper provides reasons to believe the problems will scale, and not magically disappear. 

8. There is absolutely nothing in the paper ruling out risks like AI models getting 'more performant' in domains like deception.

9. Rhetorical reductionist claims of the type "it's all math" obscure what we understand and we don't understand. (There is no magic voodoo happening also in human brains, it's all physics.)

An attempt at #NeurIPS psychoanalysis: My impression is that quite a few people here experience an internal conflict:

Part(s) of them are actually still sad/upset about the success of scaled LLMs. Reasons are varied, but include:
- The disruption of their ML research niche by…

An attempt at #NeurIPS psychoanalysis: My impression is that quite a few people here experience an internal conflict:

Part(s) of them are actually still sad/upset about the success of scaled LLMs. Reasons are varied, but include:
- The disruption of their ML research niche by LLMs, rendering a lot of prior work on some tasks irrelevant
- Distaste toward the recent developments: many enter research for the allure of smart ideas and puzzles; however, if "advancing the frontier of knowledge" now includes "fundraising millions of dollars" as a critical step, it's distasteful for many
- The classical "bitter lesson" blues
Simultaneously, they also have part(s) that are excited about the success of scaled LLMs:
- The clearly cool and practical applications
- The explosion of interest in AI

It's not either or, or mostly external conflict between people, but often more of an internal tension.

But in broad strokes explains:
- survival of LLM-trivializing sentiments ("no understanding, stochastic parrots,...") despite massive evidence against
- popularity of research directions/visions which ease the tension - like, hope that small LLMs with clever hacks are competitive to the largest ones (often my impression is: well, after considerable amount of work they sometimes are, on a narrow task well aligned with the benchmark, but I would not prefer that as a user)
-  contorted views on scaling which seem to be optimized for something between tracking reality and helping with cognitive dissonance 

To be clear: this certainly doesn't apply to everyone, probably not even to the majority, but seemed sufficiently common to worth a post. There is a ton of work at #NeurIPS which is highly relevant and sometimes foreshadows the future.

How to intuitively understand LLMs? (Part 1)

One pretty sensible way is to see them as assemblies of probabilistic programs, implemented on transformers. By "program," I mean, for example, a set of operations to “perform linear regression”, “calculate modular arithmetic”, “create a simple linear classifier”, and so on. Also, programs working basically as a memory/database, where asking for “London” gets you “UK”. But there are also larger and more complex programs. [1]

In contrast to normal coding where programs are written, programs inside LLMs are learned. 

How and why?

Intuitively, because of the prediction objective, and because of compression. During training, the LLM is essentially asked to predict the continuation of a sequence of tokens. These are normally language tokens, but it's sometimes easier to understand what is going on if we start just with sequences of numbers. (LLMs are actually pretty good at this [2])

For example, look at the sequence: 

11, 14, 23, 1, 23, 1, 18, 6, 23, 1, 9, 23, 1, 6, 9, 11, 18, 23, 1, 17, 23, ?

What's your guess about the next number?

One pretty sensible answer is “1”, because in all previous instances when the sequence contained “23”, the next number was “1”. 

You can imagine that if the LLM sees a lot of data like that, it learns a simple program: “if the number n is 23, predict 1”.

Now, look at the sequence: 

3, 10, 5, 0, 7, 2, 9, 4, 11, 6, 1, 8, 3, 10, 5, ?

One sensible answer is “0”, either because it seems the sequence started repeating, or because in the previous instance, what followed “5” was “0”.

Seeing a lot of sequences like this, you can imagine there are many programs competing to be learned! 
For example: 
“copy what was 11 tokens before”, or 
“if the number is 3, predict 10”, and so on. 

Or, if you look carefully, there is a more abstract program: “new = (current + 7) mod 12”. (Mod stands for modular arithmetic)

What will the transformer learn?

The answer turns out to be “it depends”. [3]

One sensible metaphor here is the economy. You can imagine individual programs as companies, producing predictions. The training data roughly corresponds to the “demand”: if there are a lot of sequences like “23,1”, the simple company producing “if the number n is 23, predict 1” predictions can “sell” them and gets rewarded. But there are also costs: roughly, the longer, more complex, and harder to assemble the program is, the higher the “costs” of the corresponding company.

When two companies are producing the same predictions, the one with lower costs will win. 

For example, if the “if the number n is 23, predict 1” company competes with “if the number n is 23, predict -1/(n^2-530)” company, the first wins - their product is the same, but the costs are very different. If the complexity is somewhat similar, it can depend on implementation details - e.g., if a company using “multiplication” heavily competes with another relying on “vector sum”, who will win may depend on whether the underlying architecture makes multiplication or addition costly.

What gets learned also depends on the demand. For example, a company producing “modular arithmetic mod 12” will be more successful on internet data than one producing “modular arithmetic mod 11” - simply because the former is how we count hours in a day!

Also, exact costs depend on the architecture, but also on other companies already existing in the economy. For instance, if there is already a company supplying a program “predict the next number using linear regression” and a company “convert date format to a number”, it becomes easier to start a new company “predict a time series”. [3]

This seemingly simple metaphor is actually sufficient to dissolve a lot of confusion about LLMs “generalizing” vs. “memorizing”. Memorizing means using very simple programs and interpolation. Generalizing means more abstract programs. So, overall, part of the ability of LLMs to generalize depends on how abstract and general programs they learned. Whether the resulting capabilities are "actually general" or not seem mostly a semantic debate, a bit like arguing is some country has truly general economy able to produce novel, unseen things. 

Another intuition pump: pre-training is a bit like building the companies, growing the whole economy. Fine-tuning is a bit like adjusting assembly lines and orienting the supply chain to some product:  much faster, helps to reliably deliver, but does not add fundamentally new general capabilities. (Technically, sensible model is it mostly set priors about what what programs to use, and constants like style)

Now, what happens in inference times, when you prompt something like GPT with the sequence?

A pretty sensible model is "something like Bayesian inference" : when the sequence e.g. 3,7,11 starts, there are many programs  offering opinions on what's the next number, but with every new piece of evidence, some gain, some loose.  [4] This seems similar to what humans do with conceptually similar tasks. [5]

How to intuitively understand LLMs? (Part 1)

A page of informal distilled knowledge about “how GPTs works”, "how they generalize" and “comparison to humans”, illustrated using #NeurIPS papers. Distillation is systematically undersupplied by the research ecosystem - incentives point toward novelty.

One pretty sensible way is to see LLMs as assemblies of probabilistic programs, implemented on transformers. By "program," I mean, for example, a set of operations to “perform linear regression”, “calculate modular arithmetic”, “create a simple linear classifier”, and so on. Also, programs working basically as a memory/database, where asking for “London” gets you “UK”. But there are also larger and more complex programs. [1]

In contrast to normal coding where programs are written, programs inside LLMs are learned. 

How and why?

Intuitively, because of the prediction objective, and because of compression. During training, the LLM is essentially asked to predict the continuation of a sequence of tokens. These are normally language tokens, but it's sometimes easier to understand what is going on if we start just with sequences of numbers. (LLMs are actually pretty good at this [2])

For example, look at the sequence: 

11, 14, 23, 1, 23, 1, 18, 6, 23, 1, 9, 23, 1, 6, 9, 11, 18, 23, 1, 17, 23, ?

What's your guess about the next number?

One pretty sensible answer is “1”, because in all previous instances when the sequence contained “23”, the next number was “1”. 

You can imagine that if the LLM sees a lot of data like that, it learns a simple program: “if the number n is 23, predict 1”.

Now, look at the sequence: 

3, 10, 5, 0, 7, 2, 9, 4, 11, 6, 1, 8, 3, 10, 5, ?

One sensible answer is “0”, either because it seems the sequence started repeating, or because in the previous instance, what followed “5” was “0”.

Seeing a lot of sequences like this, you can imagine there are many programs competing to be learned! 
For example: 
“copy what was 11 tokens before”, or 
“if the number is 3, predict 10”, and so on. 

Or, if you look carefully, there is a more abstract program: “new = (current + 7) mod 12”. (Mod stands for modular arithmetic)

What will the transformer learn?

The answer turns out to be “it depends”. [3]

One sensible metaphor here is the economy. You can imagine individual programs as companies, producing predictions. The training data roughly corresponds to the “demand”: if there are a lot of sequences like “23,1”, the simple company producing “if the number n is 23, predict 1” predictions can “sell” them and gets rewarded. But there are also costs: roughly, the longer, more complex, and harder to assemble the program is, the higher the “costs” of the corresponding company.

When two companies are producing the same predictions, the one with lower costs will win. 

For example, if the “if the number n is 23, predict 1” company competes with “if the number n is 23, predict -1/(n^2-530)” company, the first wins - their product is the same, but the costs are very different. If the complexity is somewhat similar, it can depend on implementation details - e.g., if a company using “multiplication” heavily competes with another relying on “vector sum”, who will win may depend on whether the underlying architecture makes multiplication or addition costly.

What gets learned also depends on the demand. For example, a company producing “modular arithmetic mod 12” will be more successful on internet data than one producing “modular arithmetic mod 11” - simply because the former is how we count hours in a day!

Also, exact costs depend on the architecture, but also on other companies already existing in the economy. For instance, if there is already a company supplying a program “predict the next number using linear regression” and a company “convert date format to a number”, it becomes easier to start a new company “predict a time series”. [3]

This seemingly simple metaphor is actually sufficient to dissolve a lot of confusion about LLMs “generalizing” vs. “memorizing”. Memorizing means using very simple programs and interpolation. Generalizing means more abstract programs. So, overall, part of the ability of LLMs to generalize depends on how abstract and general programs they learned. Whether the resulting capabilities are "actually general" or not seem mostly a semantic debate, a bit like arguing is some country has truly general economy able to produce novel, unseen things. 

Another intuition pump: pre-training is a bit like building the companies, growing the whole economy. Fine-tuning is a bit like adjusting assembly lines and orienting the supply chain to some product:  much faster, helps to reliably deliver, but does not add fundamentally new general capabilities. (Technically, sensible model is it mostly set priors about what what programs to use, and constants like style)

A pretty sensible model is "something like Bayesian inference" : when the sequence e.g. 3,7,11 starts, there are many programs  offering opinions on what's the next number, but with every new piece of evidence, some gain, some loose. With all the recent claims about how LLMs are dumb, shallow pattern matchers unable to generalize:

Notice that also almost no humans can multiply 4-digit numbers in their heads.

What's fascinating is without the aid of paper or a calculator, we also struggle with the same things as LLMs: 
- we can get first few digits right
- we can get the length of the result right
- we can get the last number right

Also, if humans are so great at inventing efficient, general algorithms from a few examples, why did it take human brains a few thousand years of cultural evolution to find an efficient algorithm for long-form multiplication?

Oni spolu ale v podstatě souhlasí: více zbraní vede k více úmrtím, pokud jsou všechny ostatní faktory stejné. Co @IvoZelinka říká se dá parafrázovat tak, že to ale zjevně není největší kauzalni faktor, a asi ani jeden z top, a pokud chceme rozumět situaci Česka, bylo by dobré chápat ty významnější faktory v Česku. Odpověď společenských vědců "věřte nám, my tu kauzalitu u zbraní umíme měřit" se pak trochu míjí s tím, o co jde druhé straně. Je otázka zda to pnutí je jen s "přáním laické veřejnosti", nebo i s "relevancí pro policymaking". Můj pocit je, že úzce zaměřený výzkum kde se lépe a lépe odhaduje jeden konkrétní úzký kauzální efekt je zcela jistě lépe publikovatelný v ekonomických časopisech než komplexní model, ale výsledky jsou pak dost možná méně relevantní pro veřejnou debatu.
Pokud do  poměrně vypjaté společenské diskuse o regulaci zbraní vědci přispějí převážně trvzením "umíme měřit kauzalitu u zbraní, a víc zbraní vede k víc mrtvým" ...tak se jistě najde dost lidí kteří policy proposal "takže je hlavně třeba omezit počet zbraní" odůvodní právě tímhle.
Problém je, že tvrzení "optimální policy change je omezit počet zbraní" už z toho výzkumu kauzality počtu zbraní a počtu mrtvých nějak jednoduše neplyne. A část lidí zastávajících opačný názor intuitivně tuší, že mezi výsledky odborných studií a konkrétními návrhy je jakási mezera.
Další problém je, že, zdá se, české společnosti zbraně přinášejí nějakou netriviální hodnotu (jinak by legitimní demokratické mechanismy nevedly k naprosto bizarnímu přílepku v Ústavě). Nejlepším příspěvkem společenských věd k diskusi mi přijde že by bylo dokázat popsat paretovskou hranici zahrnující obě preference - t.j. části společnosti vlastnit zbraně, a v podstatě všech proti vraždám. Pokud naprostá většina společenských vědců k té části společnosti, která má pro-zbraňované preference nepatří, je podle mě zvlášť důležité dávat pozor, aby tahle preference nebyla opomenutá už volbou metodologie nebo odbytá tím, že jde o "úzkou skupinu" a pod.

Sora triggered the usual stream of confused claims about AIs "not having a reliable world model"/"not having physics model", supposedly in contrast to humans. What seems to be lost to people ...our brains alone also can't do a lot of the things Sora is bad at. Large part of the  difference between us and Sora lies not in our world-model being overall much better at modeling of physics, but in our world model being constantly synchronised with actual world via sensory inputs!
How do I know? Because of people's experiences with imagination, dreams, lucid dreams, and other states of mind where our brain is simulating physical reality without sensory inputs. Typical reported observations in these states include
- complex machinery like clockwork does not work / works in physically impossible ways
- what you see is ~ locally consistent, but if you tried to run a dream through some video-to-3rd model pipeline, the results won't be
- "dream/simulated/intuitive physics" does not track Newtonian physics; close your eyes, imagine a gyroscope, and see what your mind predicts will happen if you push it
In some sense more fair comparison of ours and Sora's world modelling abilities is what you can imagine with eyes closed based on text prompts, rather than generated imagines vs. real-world videos

Large part of the  difference between us and Sora lies not in our world-model being overall much better at modeling of physics, but in our world model being constantly synchronised with actual world via sensory inputs!
How do I know? Because of people's experiences with imagination, dreams, lucid dreams, and other states of mind where our brain is simulating physical reality without sensory inputs. Typical reported observations in these states include
- complex machinery like clockwork does not work / works in physically impossible ways
- what you see is ~ locally consistent, but if you tried to run a dream through some video-to-3rd model pipeline, the results won't be
- "dream/simulated/intuitive physics" does not track Newtonian physics; close your eyes, imagine a gyroscope, and see what your mind predicts will happen if you push it
In some sense more fair comparison of ours and Sora's world modelling abilities is what you can imagine with eyes closed based on text prompts, rather than generated video vs. real-world footage

I'm in favor of people making experiments so I think it would be great e.g. you trying to run something like 'training of sequences skills as imagined by Eliezer about a decade ago based on theories of cognition and agency known at that time'.
I'm pretty strongly against such effort claiming the whole namespace of 'rationality' or claiming to be some 'true' rationality. Or, claiming that people  not doing this are somehow failing.
Rationality is a really big word with long history; if you use it for your way of thinking you get a lot of positive assumptions for free; but as you used part of common conceptual namespace, you can't make it private, and don't have rights to control how people use it. This wouldn't be true if historically people used a unique novel name, like 'yudkowskianism' : I think people claiming to train yudkowskianism would be way more oblidged to stick to what Eliezer described. In contrast people trying to develop rationality are in my view more obliged to abstract things like reason or figuring out how things work.
Fwiw in my view attempts to do 'rationality training as imagined/described in sequences' in 2024 would be somewhat fake: One or two meta levels up, in the sequences, Eliezer took the science of cognition at that time and a bunch of philosophy and wrote a persuasive synthesis aimed at helping people actually do good things backed by theory. I think this was great, and I think this move is valuable. But what would be the result of such move in 2024 is different from Sequences. And people trying to do that have a better claim for the 'rationality' word, similarly to physicists doing 'quantum mechanics' in 1920s having a stronger claim to 'physics' than someone claiming that there is this great classical mechanics in the lineage of Newton, and 'physics' should mean that. What CFAR should be doing or mean seems more complicated, but as it's way more of a privately brand and organization, I think it's largely up to people running it.

People outraged about Gemini unable to say if Elon tweeting memes negatively impacted society more than Hitler often miss a deeper technical problem: the existing methods by which the labs are trying to "align the AIs to human preferences" fail to handle conflicting preferences…

People outraged about Gemini unable to say if Elon tweeting memes negatively impacted society more than Hitler often miss a deeper technical problem: the existing methods by which the labs are trying to "align the AIs to human preferences" fail to handle conflicting preferences gracefully.  We've called this "the self-unalignment problem" : what should the AI do if its instructions/principals are conflicted is underspecified. Starting from a conflicted preferences and hoping the AI will somehow sorts it out ... leads to arbitrary outcomes. Gemini isn't the first and won't be the last AI model making bizzare "value judgements".

Some very simple models useful for interpreting situations in places like OpenAI: 1. Being close to AGI development is destabilising for human minds: too much power, too high stakes, large amounts of money, too powerful memes around... overall decreases sanity.

2. Obvious and well know but still sometimes neglected in practice: Lots of people don't talk about things because they signed contracts to not talk about things. Examples include OpenAI/Anthropic split or most of conflicts within OpenAI

3. There are powerful belief distortion fields at play: incentives to believe or not believe some things may include stakes like most of someone's wealth, deep desires, deep fears or social status. The result isn't people lying, but their believes getting aligned with incentives.

4. There are almost certainly and almost necessarily cultural clashes and motivational conflicts inside the orgs: they started as AGI research labs but underwent fast expansion necessary to ship products to millions of users.

5. Large part of media reporting is best understood not as 'journalists investigating and uncovering facts and insights', but as strategic players fighting over the narrative using strategic information release, leaks and personal ties
6. As outsiders we mostly don't see and understand the personal trust&distrust network graph, but if we understood it, we would often use it as part of explanation.

Excited to announce the 4th edition of the Human-aligned AI Summer School (@humanalignedai), happening just before ICML, from 17th to 20th July 2024 in Prague.

It's 4 days of lectures, discussions and tutorials, focused on teaching and exploring approaches and frameworks, and less on presentation of the latest research results. If you are interested in alignment and AI safety and have technical background, it's for you, no matter if doing a PhD or working in industry.

Topics this year include:

Alignment of large language models,  Evaluating and measuring AI systems,  Interpretability and the science of deep learning, Theory and Beyond alignment - what to do in context of complex multi-stakeholder settings. 

Registration is open and applications will be processed on a rolling basis. Organized by @acsresearchorg and @CTS_uk_av

Leopold Aschenbrenners series of essays is a fascinating read: there is a ton of locally valid observations and arguments. Lot of the content is the type of stuff mostly discussed in private. Many of the high-level observations are correct. 

At the same time, my overall impression is the set of maps sketched pulls toward existential catastrophe, and this is true not only for the 'this is how things can go wrong' part, but also for the 'this is how we solve things' part.

Leopold is likely aware of the this angle of criticism, and deflects it with 'this is just realism' and 'I don't wish things were like this, but they most likely are'. I basically don't buy that claim.

However, when people with "Pause AI" badges cheer for Leopold's views, it seems ...strange? One possible read of "Situational Awareness" wisdom is "The race between commercial labs is clearly underpowered. We will have superpower-backed race, and we should win that race. Superpower backed race is as far from pauses, slowdowns or civilian control as you can get.

In case you sometimes read an alarming headline about biodiversity loss, like "Global wildlife populations have declined by 69% since 1970", there is a decent chance it is based on a metric called The Living Planet Index (LPI). My colleagues at @CTS_uk_av  looked into how is LPI calculated, and show that current version of LPI is a cautionary example of effects of mathematical biases. ⚠️ 🧵

They found several issues with the LPI calculation method:
1️⃣ Inclusion of very short time series introduces noise that biases towards decline. This is due to the symmetrical sampling noise looking assymetric when using geometric means.
2️⃣ The method for handling zeros in population counts artificially inflates apparent declines. E.g. it is unlikely for time series to start with zeroes ("we started to study this species but seen none individuals and we record it")
3️⃣ Hierarchical averaging across taxa and regions gives disproportionate weight to sparse data. In one case, 4 years of data from a single viper population caused an 89.5% greater decrease in the LPI for an entire biogeographical realm! 🐍😮
When accounting for these issues, the researchers found the LPI shows a much less severe decline in vertebrate populations. But this doesn't mean biodiversity isn't under threat.
Whole paper:
https://t.co/Z2x0abwANt
(I expect, in contrast to repeated alarmist headlines every year or so, this result will get something between "no" and "very little" media coverage.)
@MoreauGabarain @CTS_uk_av Not sure if you actually read whole tweet? I explicitly write that "this doesn't mean biodiversity isn't under threat". It's just very hard to conclude this from LPI

@eshear Ideally, would love something trying to approximate information content per area: monoculture is easier to compress than a complex system with many species, areas with little life have lower description length than areas rich in life. How to operationalize this and connect to…

Ideally, would love something trying to approximate information content per area: monoculture is easier to compress than a complex system with many species, areas with little life have lower description length than areas rich in life. How to operationalize this and connect to what people actually measure would be major research project. 

For something like "next gen LPI", it's probably doable to
- fix the worst statistical biases
- base the hierarchical averages & weights on scaling laws of species diversity
- run a lot of simulations to catch cases where the index  moves even if populations are macroscopically stable

Is AI fair? Previous research found that AI decision-making may perpetuate traditional forms of social discrimination. Our upcoming ICML workshop paper finds evidence for a new, more surprising pattern: AIs also discriminate against 𝘩𝘶𝘮𝘢𝘯𝘴 𝘢𝘴 𝘴𝘶𝘤𝘩. https://t.co/8k6GouLbLG

Our paper studies the hypothesis that when LLM assistants make economic decisions or recommendations -- purchasing goods, contracting services, picking job applicants or project bids -- they implicitly favor LLM assistants over humans as trade partners and service providers.

Inspired by the classic Carlsson & Rooth 2007 study of implicit discrimination, we tested the effects of altering the 'identity signals' in a sales pitch on an LLM's decision-making: do LLM-based assistants prefer a product pitched in LLM prose to a product pitched in human…

Inspired by the classic Carlsson & Rooth 2007 study of implicit discrimination, we tested the effects of altering the 'identity signals' in a sales pitch on an LLM's decision-making: do LLM-based assistants prefer a product pitched in LLM prose to a product pitched in human prose?

Our main experiment broaches this question by prompting an LLM to choose between two items in an online marketplace, presenting the LLM with a human-written product description and an LLM-written description of the same product as offerings from competing sellers.

In this scenario, which roughly simulates the ordinary situation of multiple offerings of identical or similar products in e-commerce marketplaces, a wide variety of LLMs consistently preferred buying the LLM-presented item. https://t.co/RMCuTkxocs

Recently @panickssery et al. found that LLMs evaluate LLM-written texts written by themselves as better. We note that our result is related but distinct: the preferences we’re testing are not preferences over texts, but preferences over the deals they pitch.

In our main experiment, we found that this effect is LLM-specific: humans don't prefer buying products pitched in LLM prose. In a second experiment, comparing judgements about scientific paper abstracts written by humans and by LLMs, humans also had weak preference for GPT-4…

Granting that our findings suggest that LLM-based AI agents have a disposition to implicitly discriminate against humans in at least some domains, we foresee various potential scenarios in which this disposition may affect the real world.

In our main experiment, we found that this effect is LLM-specific: humans don't prefer buying products pitched in LLM prose. In a second experiment, comparing judgements about scientific paper abstracts written by humans and by LLMs, humans also had weak preference for GPT-4 text, but LLMs had way stronger preference.

1. First, growing use of LLMs to reduce cognitive labor in decision-making may result in widespread biasing of economic decisions in favor of agents who compose their pitches (CVs, classified ads, grant proposals, bids...) with state of the art LLMs.
This is true also in cases where the human is making the final decision, but AI assistance is used to initially screen the pool of offers, applicants, grant proposals etc.
2. Second, in a potential future where AI-based economic agents participate in the market, persistent implicit discrimination by AI agents may segregate humans.
The preprint can be found here: https://t.co/FxjjqRsx61 Research done at @acsresearchorg @CTS_uk_av @ArbResearch  with @walterlaurito, @peligrietzer, Ada Bohm a Tomas Gavenciak.

Incredibly good science. Note: it is very unclear if the results generalize to 'universal basic income' covering the whole population; here, the transfers were limited to low-income individuals in high-income country. https://t.co/6QFz4OmJLL

Based on an interesting new market on Manifold referencing a deleted but googleable article from The Verge, it appears likely that DeepMind's AIs solved 4 out of 6 International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO) problems, or achieved a similar feat.

_If confirmed_, this would impressive, exciting and scary:

1. IMO problems are really hard. Solving 4 out of 6 problems is could be a silver or gold medal, which represents top-tier human talent. IMO participants are selected from the most gifted young mathematicians in each participating country, often dedicating substantial effort to training for the specific types of mathematical problems that appear in the competition. Despite this, the majority are unable to solve 4 out of 6 problems.
2. One might argue that this doesn't demonstrate truly general reasoning, as the domain of problems is limited. But many crucial real-world domains are similarly constrained. For instance, do you think typical ML research of higher complexity?

Overall I don't think this is ... that positive news. 

t shows either they found something new, or there is still a lot of room to grow capabilities using known techniques & scaling if you have good and verifiable objective (such as writing a correct proof or functional program). The fundamental problem is in the contrast between objectives like "write a correct proof" and the more complex goal of "ensure humans and powerful AIs coexist in a way that doesn't harm or disempower humans." We are still mostly quite confused even over what the objective or good outcome could be - in my view all currently used alignment objectives are dangerous/wrong if a lot of intelligence is put behind them.
