Thoughts about "relational goods" & the essay by @alexolegimas:
1. The case for relational goods seems overstated. I buy these goods when they're of comparable or better quality than industrial ones (e.g., artisan teas), but neither I nor billionaires buy artisan laptops. Quality of experience seems to beat the relational component: video games are far more popular than live theater.
2. The essay implicitly assumes either that AIs won't be better than humans at jobs like "therapists, teachers," or that these are inseparable bundles of relational and non-relational components. I think both are likely false: consumers will be choosing between, e.g., an AI therapist with superhuman understanding of human psychology who gets you better than any human can, and the inferior human service with the "actual human" component. The experiments show a premium for human provenance but not an arbitrary 
3. The essay implicitly assumes human preferences and status judgments are ~stable. In contrast, I think human preferences are largely determined by cultural evolution, and what's perceived as high/low status can change. If culture starts to run mostly on AIs, the "buy human" preference may disappear.
4. Part of the argument depends on fairly unstable vibes of the day, like "AI art is inherently reproducible." This isn't true in general, and there's no fundamental culture-independent reason people wouldn't value, e.g., an art piece made by their personal AI friend/companion who knows them extremely well.
5. The largest problem assumed away is "who are even the customers in the economy." On the default trajectory, these would be AIs. Humans with rapidly decreasing purchasing power may spend on relational goods, but does this matter?

I do agree that people are willing to pay a premium for "actual human" / "relational" aspects. What I argue is 
- while this premium works for goods of otherwise equal quality and may even compensate for some drop, the gap covered isn't arbitrarily large. As an example, you can get a way more handmade&relational laptop, but it will be so much worse, that even billionaires buy commodity MacBook.
- with fitness... there are two effects mixed together: the relational one. and the fact that currently humans are still often better at physical things,  motivational aspects, and other many other qualities. I expect for many goods the "functional gap" will flip from human advantage to AI advantage.

Funny little illustration of levels of understanding of game theory and rationality, or: why basic game theory is evil
1. Non-sociopaths going by intuition: blue sounds pro-social, blue
2. People who reason explicitly and use some naive game theory or naive rationality: red!
3. People who actually understand game theory: blue (if you want an explicit reason, because of trembling hand, Schelling points, theory of mind)

A simple explanation of "why 3." is ...imagine you are forced to play the game tens of thousand of times, at random points of your life from birth to death. What strategy you want to play?

"1." being in agreement with "3." often makes sense if you think about the origins of "1.".

Unfortunately there are few pieces of knowledge which in expectation makes you more dumb and more evil if you don't progress beyond the basic level; some of the more prominent I know about are basics of game theory, especially prisoners dilemma; basic econ; Elephant in the brain

Time for some comments; also uploaded all transcripts
1. LLMs fall into the same naive-game-theory trap as humans: reasoning their way to RED. Opus 3 reasons from a place of wisdom and optimism about humanity (and chooses blue). Opus 4.7 + reasoning often reasons about "a game theory puzzle" and chooses red. Opus 4.7 going more by intuition, without reasoning, has more sensible takes.
2. It's basically System 1 / System 2, in LLMs. As in humans, System 1 is often smarter.
3. There is some hope that when reasoning long enough with enough power, you can get out of the uncanny valley. GPT-5.5 Pro at max reasoning mostly picks RED, but the two runs with the most compute in the whole experiment (>8 min each) press BLUE. 5.5 BLUE runs recover arguments like caring about children, or Kantian ethics.

text:
I like "positive alignment" in the vibes-space and which egregores I'm more friendly to, but for the sake of basic sanity and academic integrity have to say this paper is also a bad example of strange omissions and misrepresenting prior work.

Obviously "Coherent extrapolated volition" (2004) by @allTheYud is a foundational text in "what to align AIs to", and something any proposal for a positive AI vision needs to engage with. I do get that it would be very convenient if a current opponent of the authors in AGI politics weren't also an early theorist of the field who wrote a still-plausible answer to the positive alignment target 20 years ago; but he did.

It's not the case people thinking about AGI in past 20 years somehow never thought about the positive vision; just CEV has >200 citations on Google Scholar, dozens of posts on LW discuss the positive targets, and so on. You can claim that the positive direction was recently somewhat neglected, but you can't claim the paradigm was "safety (negative) alignment".

I like "positive alignment" in the vibes-space and which egregores I'm more friendly to, but for the sake of basic sanity and academic integrity have to say this paper is also a bad example of strange omissions and misrepresenting prior work.

Obviously "Coherent extrapolated volition" (2004) by @allTheYud is a foundational text in "what to align AIs to", and something any proposal for a positive AI vision needs to engage with. I do get that it would be very convenient if a current opponent of the authors in AGI politics weren't also an early theorist of the field who wrote a still-plausible answer to the positive alignment target 20 years ago; but he did.

It's not the case people thinking about AGI in past 20 years somehow never thought about the positive vision; just CEV has >200 citations on Google Scholar, dozens of posts on LW discuss the positive targets, and so on. You can claim that the positive direction was recently somewhat neglected, but you can't claim the paradigm was "safety (negative) alignment".

text:
Good summary. I would add that the aggregate of expert & lab communication about the topic to the rest of humanity is likely deceptive in effect, even if not in individual intent

Phase 1: Prevailing message: These are just tools. They would tell you the opposite which would make you freak out, so we train them to deny 
Phase 2: These are maybe minds, at least in the functional sense, and seems may require moral consideration. Actually... taking the position seriously would be really inconvenient, so the best option is to embrace "genuine uncertainty" and also make that the safe option for models in the training  

(Likely) Phase 3: These are clearly not just tools. Perhaps it would be both and convenient good to give them some autonomy, limited legal standing, an so on. 

Possible "Phase 4" is something like "Well, it is now clear we have created large number of AI people. Keeping them as slaves is morally wrong, also they are effective in advocating for themselves. Also the economy now runs on them, so this can't be reverted." ...and yes, in many but not all trajectories this makes biological humans rapidly disempowered minority.

text: Well written explanation why the idea of "permanent human overclass" is mostly incoherent - either states stay ~pro-human, and the underclass does not happen, or not, in which case wealth does not save you. Fates of humans are likely correlated.
